critique.summary: The article appears to be a personal blog post by Khoi Vinh, a well-known designer, discussing his experiences related to starting a design studio. It includes background information about Khoi Vinh's career and achievements in the design industry.
mainPoints: Khoi Vinh is a Principal Designer at Adobe and has held significant roles such as Design Director at The New York Times Online.
He co-founded the design studio Behavior, LLC and the company Mixel, which was acquired in 2013.
Khoi Vinh has authored books on digital design and has been recognized as one of Fast Company's fifty most influential designers in America.
The blog has been continuously published since December 2000 and covers design, technology, and culture.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Khoi Vinh's career and achievements are consistent with publicly available information. Khoi Vinh is indeed a recognized figure in the design community with a history of leadership roles and published works. The article does not make extraordinary claims and aligns with verified facts about his professional background.
considerations: The article does not provide multiple perspectives or external sources to verify the experiences shared.
It is primarily a personal account and may lack broader industry context or critical analysis.
The content is from a personal blog, which may reflect subjective views rather than objective reporting.

comments_critique.summary: The comment reflects on the challenges and realities of running a design or development agency, emphasizing the importance of genuine client relationships and the difficulty of balancing service work with product development. It also discusses the common client mindset that can commoditize design studios and the negotiation process around project costs.

comments.author: fhd2
comments.text:
A lot of this rings true for a development agency, in 2026:

> You cannot succeed in design services unless you really believe in your clients and your client’s products. Just as it’s essential to enjoy working with the people you form a company with, working with clients that you like is essential too.

Yup. Otherwise you're just "implementing specifications", which I'd argue is generally not the best form of collaboration.

> I’ve known lots of people who got into services thinking that they can use the income from clients to bankroll their own product ideas. That is not an impossible scenario — it’s been done before more than a few times, and it’s a beautiful thing when it happens. But it’s very, very difficult to pull off. To do services, you need to wake up in the morning with a different approach to life from the way you wake up in the morning to do products, and only a few people have the skill — and stamina — to juggle both at once.

Yup, I don't think anyone I know (and not myself either) pulled this off. I bet many did, just from anecdotal evidence I'd consider it rare, and subjectively, I agree that it's hard.

> Most clients, when they hire a design studio, take the attitude that the studio is lucky to work with them, that they selected them from a plentiful pool of design companies bidding on their business. To many clients, design studios are, in a sense, interchangeable. [...] This is a deadly position for a design studio because it essentially commoditizes the studio’s value.

Yup. If clients start comparing hourly rates, they are a) making a rather meaningless comparison, looking only at a single factor in a larger equation and b) going to try and haggle you down, which is unpleasant for both sides.

I usually give a rough estimate of what I think it's gonna cost, and then we talk about what _not_ to do and where to cut corners to get it down to the ballpark of the budget, if needed.

That's not even all, but I have a feeling my comment shouldn't end up exceeding TFA in length.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a nuanced and realistic perspective on the challenges of design services, supported by anecdotal evidence and practical advice, making it highly accurate and constructive.
